raga.name: Bhairavi
raga.description: Called the 'Queen of Ragas'. Uses all komal (flat) svaras except Sa and Pa. Extremely versatile — traditionally the concluding raga of any concert. Evokes deep devotion, compassion, and a bittersweet tenderness.

forbidden.name: Shuddh Rishabh
forbidden.reason: Bhairavi uses only komal Re. Shuddh Re would shift the raga towards Kafi or Khamaj thaat.

forbidden.name: Shuddh Gandhar
forbidden.reason: Bhairavi's character depends on komal Ga. Shuddh Ga would change the mood entirely.

movement.aroha: S r g m P d n S'
movement.avaroha: S' n d P m g r S
pakad: d n S' n d P
movement.pakad_string: g m d P, m g r S, d n S' n d P

characteristic_phrases.context: Opening — gentle oscillation around Sa establishing komal territory
Ascending through the heart of the raga
Signature Bhairavi movement — skip from Ma to komal Dha

characteristic_phrases.phrase: d n S' n d P
characteristic_phrases.context: Upper register exploration with characteristic descent
Resolution — classic Bhairavi cadence to Sa

note_rules.Sa_approach: Approached gently from komal Re (r S) — the komal Re gives Bhairavi its distinctive plaintive quality
note_rules.Ma_treatment: Always shuddh. A resting note and pivot between lower and upper tetrachords
note_rules.Pa_role: Strong anchor in the upper register — phrases often orbit around Pa
note_rules.komal_character: The four komal notes (r, g, d, n) together create the deeply emotional Bhairavi atmosphere

vadi.role: King note — the emotional anchor. Extended treatment in alaap, phrases frequently rest on Ma.

samvadi.role: Queen note — Sa itself serves as samvadi, giving Bhairavi its grounded, devotional quality.

time.prahar: Fourth prahar of night / early morning

context.aesthetic: Evokes the tenderness of early morning — dew on leaves, temple bells, the ache of separation. The all-komal palette creates an emotional depth unmatched by brighter ragas.

western_equivalent.relationship: The Phrygian mode matches the Bhairavi thaat. The b2 (komal Re) is the defining colour — also found in Flamenco and Middle Eastern music.

lofi.notes: The minor/Phrygian tonality creates a naturally melancholic, introspective mood perfect for lofi. Komal Ga and komal Ni add emotional depth over mellow beats.

ambient.notes: Bhairavi is arguably the most ambient-compatible raga. Its devotional stillness and komal palette create deeply meditative soundscapes.

calm.notes: The gentle, komal-heavy scale naturally produces calm textures. Works beautifully at slow tempos with sustained notes.

upbeat.notes: Less natural fit for upbeat styles due to the minor tonality, but Bhairavi thumris can be lively. Requires careful arrangement.

jazz_fusion.notes: Phrygian-inflected scale offers exotic tension for modal solos; melancholic compassion fits bluesy feels.

cinematic.notes: Deep emotional pathos ideal for dramatic scoring and closers.

downtempo.notes: Versatile komal palette creates intimate organic grooves.

electronic.notes: Proven in electronic disco; all-komal phrases adapt to synth arps.

chillhop.notes: Melancholic lines pair with walking bass warmth.

reggae_dub.notes: Compassionate mood suits dub delay and deep bass.

trap.notes: Can translate into dark hooks; best as short, memorable motifs with 808 glides echoing raga bends.

rock.notes: Phrygian/minor-rock language is familiar; use bends and slides to preserve the raga character.

fusion_notes: survives acceleration better than heavier ragas
Treat Bhairavi as 'high flexibility': you can allow tasteful chromatic approach tones, but keep the core melodic gravity intact.
Because Bhairavi is often a winding-down/closing raga, an earlier climax + long outro/fade feels stylistically natural.
If the production is busy, preserve identity by anchoring repeated cadences on stable resting tones (especially around Sa/Ma).
